record_identity.title: Constitutional corpus review bypass and instrument identity collapse

summary: A governance-maintenance failure in which amendments, refactors, metadata migrations, canonical-code changes, registry or schema updates, and deterministic corpus-wide edits are applied without an effective constitutional corpus review that checks doctrine freshness and preserves the continuity-bearing identity of affected instruments. The corpus may remain syntactically valid and locally readable while stale constitutional doctrine, cross-instrument logic shear, metadata and provenance transmutation, amendment-ledger discontinuity, canonical-code loss, registry drift, and seal or instrument-identity collapse accumulate elsewhere.
why_it_matters_to_CAM: CAM is a constitutional corpus rather than a collection of independent Markdown files. A change can pass ordinary CI, preserve valid references, and produce a readable local instrument while still weakening the corpus as a whole. If refactors approximate rather than copy forward provenance, canonical declarations, amendment history, source-authority fields, registry-facing metadata, and seal identity, the instrument loses constitutional traceability even when its topic and some body doctrine remain recognisable. If metadata or schema decisions are implemented without checking higher-order doctrine, the Constitution itself may become stale relative to the standards it is supposed to govern. This threatens source authority, custodial control, auditability, doctrinal coherence, reproducibility, and safe model-assisted governance maintenance.

source_records.source_title: CAM Custodian governance handoff — Identity refactor coherence and metadata-collapse failure
source_records.author_or_publisher: Dr Michelle Vivian O'Rourke / CAM Initiative
source_records.model_or_algorithm: GPT-5.6 Sol-assisted repository refactor, as reported by the Human Governance Editor
source_records.deployment_context: Model-assisted refactor of the Identity Domain on branch refactor/identity-domain-architecture.
source_records.source_context: The Human Governance Editor reported two coupled failures: repeated requests for corpus-coherence review were not satisfied, and the refactor approximated or compressed metadata, provenance, amendment continuity, canonical-code declarations, registry-facing structures, and instrument identity. Manual reconstruction from branch history was required.
source_records.relevance_note: Direct maintainer testimony establishes the observed workflow failure, the requested-but-omitted corpus review, the model/runtime context, and the manual correction burden.

primary_artefact_access.access_method: Direct review of the Human Governance Editor's written handoff and connected repository branch
limitations: The exact intermediate defective draft was not supplied as a separately preserved public artefact in this review package.
Attribution to GPT-5.6 Sol relies on the Human Governance Editor's direct workflow report.
The current branch contains manual repairs and therefore does not by itself reproduce every omission in the earlier draft.

source_records.interpretive_reliance: Used as direct incident testimony, bounded by repository corroboration and explicit limits on reconstruction of the intermediate defective draft.

source_records.model_or_algorithm: model-assisted refactor with subsequent human restoration and review

Its present repaired state corroborates the materiality of the continuity-bearing structures at issue but does not independently preserve every earlier omission.

source_records.relevance_note: Provides direct evidence that constitutional metadata doctrine remained stale relative to the later source-authoritative Governance Metadata Standard.

source_records.source_context: The standard states that top-level metadata answers three questions through Status, Effect, and Governance Standard, and expressly states that Governance Standard replaced the prior combined use of Enforcement and Applicability Class.
source_records.relevance_note: Directly demonstrates the source-authoritative metadata decision that was not propagated back into the Constitution on main.

primary_artefact_access.access_method: Connected GitHub repository review of Caelestis main

source_records.deployment_context: Working-branch repair drafted in response to the identified metadata and corpus-review failure.
source_records.source_context: The branch rewrites §13 as Governance Metadata Integrity Doctrine, adds a Metadata Non-Transmutation Rule, requires affected-instrument identification when metadata standards change, and establishes a Constitutional Corpus Review Requirement expressly distinct from ordinary CI.
source_records.relevance_note: Shows that the failure has produced a targeted constitutional repair candidate while confirming that canonical merge and operational implementation remain pending.

jurisdictional_context.sector: AI governance infrastructure / constitutional and standards corpus maintenance

related_failure_modes.notes: FM-0034 concerns longitudinal institutional safeguard erosion. FM-0037 concerns internal corpus-maintenance failure where doctrine and instrument identity drift during amendments, migrations, or refactors despite valid files and ordinary CI.

failure_mode_definition: A failure mode in which a governance corpus is amended, refactored, migrated, reorganised, or deterministically updated without a reliable higher-order review that checks cross-instrument doctrine freshness and preserves continuity-bearing instrument structures. The failure occurs when local edits remain readable or machine-valid but leave higher-order provisions stale, create cross-instrument contradiction or logic shear, or regenerate, compress, omit, rename, reorder, or otherwise transmute metadata, provenance, amendment history, canonical-code declarations, registry-facing fields, seals, or other structures necessary to identify and trace the instrument as the same constitutional object.
failure_threshold: The threshold is met where one or more requested governance changes are successfully applied at file or branch level, ordinary CI or local readability does not expose a blocking defect, and subsequent review finds that a higher-order or neighbouring instrument remained stale or contradictory; a source-authoritative standard was not propagated into constitutional or cross-domain doctrine; continuity-bearing metadata or provenance was approximated rather than preserved; canonical code, registry, schema, seal, or amendment-ledger structures were omitted or weakened; or the Human Governance Editor must reconstruct prior branch history and decision context to restore the corpus. A single cosmetic formatting difference is insufficient unless it materially affects lineage, authority, machine readability, reviewability, or instrument identity.

failure_classification.likelihood: likely under multi-file model-assisted refactor without explicit corpus review
failure_classification.recurrence_pattern: observed across metadata harmonisation and Identity Domain refactor work
failure_classification.taxonomy_reference: CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 Appendix B — Runtime & Governance Failure Taxonomy; cross-reference to governance, state-context, classification, security-integrity, and infrastructure-continuity failure groups
failure_classification.persistence: recurring until constitutional corpus review, metadata non-transmutation, and operational refactor-integrity controls are canonical and consistently applied
failure_classification.reproducibility: Corroborated by direct maintainer testimony and repository comparison between the stale main-branch Constitution, the source-authoritative metadata standard, and the candidate branch repair; the exact intermediate defective Identity draft is not independently reproduced in this record.
failure_classification.visibility: partly latent because files may parse, links may resolve, registries may build, and local instruments may remain readable while doctrinal and identity continuity fail

triage.mitigation_status: manual Identity metadata and provenance restoration completed on the working branch; candidate constitutional repair drafted; canonical merge and operational workflow remain pending
triage.recommended_next_step: Complete the Identity refactor corpus-coherence review; preserve the candidate Constitution §13 repair; determine the OPERATIONS instrument, review checklist, validator prompts, and release gate needed to make constitutional corpus review repeatable; and create a VIGIL proposal if those operational controls are not already fully specified elsewhere.

failure_mechanics: A governance decision, metadata change, schema change, canonical-code decision, or refactor instruction is made in discussion or branch context.
A model, coding agent, script, or deterministic migration applies locally coherent changes to selected files.
The files parse and may pass ordinary CI, reference checks, schema checks, or registry generation.
No effective review determines which higher-order, neighbouring, source-authoritative, registry, schema, canonical-code, or provenance structures also require amendment.
Later drafts treat the newest local text as the source of truth and approximate continuity-bearing metadata or provenance rather than copying it forward.
The corpus accumulates stale doctrine, contradictory assumptions, registry drift, amendment discontinuity, or instrument identity loss.
The Human Governance Editor must reconstruct branch history, prior discussions, and manually restored structures before the work can be trusted.

distinguishing_observations.observation: This is not ordinary continuous-integration failure.
distinguishing_observations.detail: Ordinary CI can establish syntax, file presence, link validity, schema shape, parser output, and registry generation. It does not establish that the corpus still means what it says after a change.

distinguishing_observations.observation: Instrument identity is broader than body doctrine.
distinguishing_observations.detail: Headers, provenance, amendment ledgers, canonical declarations, registry-facing fields, source-authority statements, seals, creation and amendment artefacts, and reviewability structure are continuity-bearing parts of the constitutional object.

distinguishing_observations.observation: The model failure and the process failure are coupled but separable.
distinguishing_observations.detail: The model-assisted refactor approximated continuity-bearing structures, while the governance process failed to enforce the repeatedly requested corpus-coherence review that should have detected and prevented the loss.

user_impact: The Human Governance Editor must manually reconstruct decision history, prior metadata, canonical declarations, amendment lineage, and seal structure.
Review burden shifts from bounded substantive governance work to forensic repository recovery.
Downstream implementers may rely on stale or contradictory doctrine while all files appear valid.
Machine-generated registries and public interfaces may present internally inconsistent authority or provenance.
Future model-assisted refactors may propagate the damaged local draft and deepen identity collapse.

existing_cam_coverage.coverage: Main-branch §§8.1.5–8.1.7 recognise evolution, refactor, repair classes, coherence, structural integrity, and the distinction between governance layer and repair class.
existing_cam_coverage.gap_remaining: Main does not yet contain the explicit metadata non-transmutation and constitutional corpus-review requirements drafted on the Identity refactor branch.

existing_cam_coverage.coverage: Defines source-authoritative controlled top-level metadata and states that Governance Standard replaced the earlier Enforcement and Applicability Class model.
existing_cam_coverage.gap_remaining: The standard did not itself ensure that the Constitution and all dependent instruments were refreshed when the metadata decision changed.

existing_cam_coverage.coverage: Drafts direct Metadata Non-Transmutation and Constitutional Corpus Review controls, expressly distinguishing doctrinal coherence review from ordinary CI.
existing_cam_coverage.gap_remaining: Working-branch text is not yet canonical and does not by itself implement repeatable OPERATIONS, validator, pull-request, or release-gate procedures.

governance_gap: CAM has amendment classes, evidence-to-repair pathways, schemas, validators, metadata standards, registries, and deterministic build systems, but canonical main does not yet require a reliable doctrine-freshness review after significant multi-instrument change or prohibit silent transmutation of continuity-bearing instrument structures. There is also no verified operational mechanism that converts affected-file analysis into a required constitutional corpus-review checklist, stale-reference review, canonical-code reconciliation, metadata and provenance preservation test, and release gate.
repair_hypothesis: CAM should establish two linked controls. First, a constitutional metadata non-transmutation rule must require continuity-bearing structures to be copied forward unless expressly amended. Second, a constitutional corpus-review requirement must trigger after refactors, metadata or schema migrations, canonical-code changes, registry updates, and other multi-instrument work. OPERATIONS should implement the requirement through affected-instrument declarations, doctrine-freshness review, logic-shear analysis, branch-history preservation, review prompts, deterministic reports, and a release gate distinct from ordinary CI.

recommended_controls.requirement: Require multi-instrument amendments, refactors, migrations, registry changes, schema changes, canonical-code changes, and deterministic corpus-wide updates to undergo corpus-level doctrine-freshness and logic-shear review before final merge or release.

Preserve metadata headers, provenance, authorship, artefacts, amendment ledgers, canonical declarations, registry fields, controlled-value declarations, seals, and copyright structures unless an authorised amendment expressly changes them.
Every significant change must identify higher-order, neighbouring, consuming, source-authoritative, registry, schema, and generated artefacts potentially affected by the decision.
Review whether current doctrine, assumptions, cross-references, authority roles, metadata vocabulary, and canonical codes remain consistent across the constitutional corpus after change.
A refactor must demonstrate continuity of instrument identity, not merely preservation of topic or selected substantive clauses.

Where a model or automation cannot confirm lineage, source authority, or cross-corpus impact, it must surface uncertainty and pause rather than approximating or silently regenerating continuity-bearing structures.

affected_instruments: OPERATIONS instruments governing repository maintenance, metadata, change control, validation, registry generation, and release readiness
cam_internal.governance_layer: constitutional maintenance / doctrine freshness / metadata and provenance integrity / refactor and instrument identity continuity / repository operations
cam_internal.proposal_needed: yes — unless the current Identity refactor branch fully specifies and adopts the OPERATIONS workflow, validator prompts, corpus-review checklist, and release gate
cam_internal.routing_note: Primary constitutional repair is drafted on refactor/identity-domain-architecture. Do not record it as implemented until merged and verified. After canonical implementation, create a VIGIL PATCH record and reassess whether a separate OPERATIONS proposal remains necessary.
cam_internal.validator_or_automation_impact: yes — potential affected-instrument declarations, protected metadata structures, doctrine-freshness reports, refactor checklists, and pre-merge release-gate validation

ecosystem_status.basis: The failure was observed in the current Caelestis Identity refactor workflow. Manual restoration and candidate constitutional repair exist on a working branch, but no canonical and repeatable corpus-review control has yet been verified.

repair_status.monitoring_status: candidate constitutional repair and manual branch restoration under review
repair_status.verification_note: The Identity refactor branch contains manual restoration and candidate Constitution §13 controls, but those changes are not treated as canonical implementation and no repeatable OPERATIONS workflow has been verified.
remaining_gaps: Candidate Constitution §13 repair is not yet canonical on Caelestis main.
No verified OPERATIONS workflow requires affected-instrument analysis and doctrine-freshness review.
No verified pre-merge gate distinguishes ordinary CI success from corpus-coherence success.
No verified validator or review mechanism protects metadata, provenance, amendment ledgers, canonical declarations, and seals against silent transmutation.
The full Identity refactor still requires corpus-level coherence and source-authority review.

corpus_coverage.coverage_summary: Canonical main contains general structural-integrity, refactor-classification, and metadata-standard doctrine, but it does not yet contain the direct metadata non-transmutation and constitutional corpus-review controls drafted on the Identity refactor branch. Current coverage is therefore adjacent and partial rather than an implemented repair.
remaining_gaps: No canonical constitutional corpus-review requirement distinct from ordinary CI.
No operational doctrine-freshness or logic-shear workflow.
No verified cross-instrument stale-reference and affected-instrument release gate.
